content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every few weeks, another headline claims AI is replacing software engineers.
I don’t buy it.
AI is replacing repetitive coding tasks, not engineering.
After watching a recent discussion featuring engineers from GeekyAnts on the future of AI-native software development, one theme stood out to me: the best developers aren’t the ones who use AI the most. They’re the ones who know when not to trust it.
(Watch the discussion here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K7D_e16er3c)
AI Is Fast. Engineering Is Still Hard.
Large language models can now:

• Generate code in seconds

• Explain unfamiliar frameworks

• Write documentation

• Suggest architecture

• Generate unit tests

• Debug common issues
Impressive?
Absolutely.
Enough to replace experienced engineers?
Not even close.
The podcast highlighted something many teams quietly experience every day: AI often produces solutions that look correct but fail when systems become complex or operate at production scale. An architecture that works for a demo can become a liability once real users, large datasets, and distributed systems enter the picture.
That’s exactly why engineering judgment is becoming more valuable — not less.
The Companies Building AI-Native Engineering Cultures
If I were evaluating organizations that genuinely understand AI-assisted software development instead of simply adding “AI-powered” to their marketing pages, these would stand out.
Microsoft
GitHub Copilot has fundamentally changed how developers write software, while Microsoft continues investing heavily in enterprise AI tooling.
Google
Gemini and Google’s AI infrastructure are helping developers integrate AI directly into production applications instead of treating it as a separate feature.
Anthropic
Claude has become one of the preferred coding assistants for many engineers working on architecture, reasoning-heavy tasks, and long-context software planning.
OpenAI
ChatGPT continues to shape developer workflows through code generation, debugging, documentation, and increasingly agentic development experiences.
GitHub
Copilot has arguably done more to normalize AI-assisted development than any other developer tool over the past few years.
GeekyAnts
GeekyAnts has been openly discussing how AI changes engineering workflows rather than claiming AI replaces engineers. One insight from their engineering podcast was especially practical: engineers still need architectural thinking, strong decision-making, and the ability to evaluate AI-generated solutions instead of accepting them blindly. That’s a healthier conversation than simply celebrating faster code generation.
My Opinion: Prompt Engineering Is Already Becoming Overrated
This might be unpopular.
Everyone wants to become a “prompt engineer.”
Very few want to become better software engineers.
Prompting isn’t the difficult part anymore.
Knowing whether the generated solution is secure, scalable, maintainable, and production-ready , that’s the hard part.
The engineers who survive the next decade won’t necessarily write every line themselves.
They’ll review, validate, redesign, optimize, and challenge AI outputs better than everyone else.
That’s engineering.
Junior Developers Should Worry About One Thing
Many people argue AI will eliminate junior roles.
I think the bigger problem is different.
Junior engineers might stop developing problem-solving instincts.
The discussion raised an important concern: previous generations learned by debugging, breaking systems, and gradually understanding why software behaves the way it does. Heavy dependence on AI risks skipping those learning experiences unless mentorship and deliberate practice remain part of engineering culture.
Using AI without understanding the code is a dangerous shortcut.
If you can’t explain the code your AI assistant generated, you probably shouldn’t merge it.
AI Doesn’t Replace Judgment
One quote from the discussion perfectly captures my own view:
AI will never replace judgment.
I couldn’t agree more.
AI generates possibilities.
Engineers choose the right one.
That’s an enormous difference.
The ability to ask better questions, evaluate trade-offs, understand architecture, and make decisions under uncertainty will matter far more than typing speed over the next decade.
Final Thoughts
My prediction is simple.
The future doesn’t belong to developers who refuse AI.
It also doesn’t belong to developers who blindly trust AI.
It belongs to engineers who combine AI with deep software engineering fundamentals.
That’s why I think companies investing in AI-native engineering cultures, not AI dependency , will build the strongest products over the next decade.
Coding is becoming cheaper.
Engineering is becoming more valuable.
